policy_statement: By 2030, implement 100% of the 2030 Agenda goals for the health sector (A7f).
mechanism_description: Implements the 2030 Agenda goals in the health sector to strengthen institutional capacity and resilience to climate-related health risks.
primary_causal_pathway: Governance/institutions → ↑ adaptive capacity & preparedness → ↓ climate-related health risk/impacts
causal_mechanism_detail:
1) Change in human activity/system: Health-sector governance and operations are strengthened through implementing a sustainability/2030 Agenda package (planning, coordination, standards, workforce capacity, surveillance, and service readiness).  
2) Mediating process: Improved institutional capability enables earlier detection of climate-sensitive health threats, better continuity of care during extremes, and more effective public health interventions (e.g., heat-health action plans, disease surveillance, emergency response).  
3) Climate-relevant outcome: Reduced morbidity/mortality and disruption from climate-related hazards (heatwaves, floods, drought-related health stresses, vector expansion), i.e., enhanced resilience/adaptive capacity.
dominant_pathway_test: The single biggest climate-relevant effect is improving health-system preparedness and response, thereby reducing climate hazard impacts on human health (i.e., lowering vulnerability rather than cutting GHGs).
mechanism_classification_reasoning:
1) Dominant causal pathway: Institutional strengthening in the health sector increases adaptive capacity, which reduces climate-related health impacts.  
2) Why this primary over alternatives: While “sustainability” agendas can include mitigation co-benefits (e.g., efficient facilities), the stated causal chain and intent are resilience to climate-related health risks—an adaptation endpoint.  
3) Edge cases applied: If a specific 2030 Agenda health action primarily targeted decarbonizing healthcare (energy efficiency, low-carbon procurement), that would shift the dominant pathway toward mitigation/resource efficiency; however, that is not inherent to all instances of this mechanism as defined (institutional adaptive capacity).  
4) Applicability to ALL instances: Any policy that implements a health-sector sustainability/2030 Agenda explicitly to strengthen institutional resilience to climate-health risks shares the same dominant adaptation pathway (capacity → reduced vulnerability).  
5) Secondary subtype assignment deferred: Any further subtyping (e.g., “health systems resilience,” “early warning,” “emergency preparedness”) depends on policy-specific evidence and is deferred to policy-level assessment.
mechanism_edge_case_notes: Some “health sustainability” implementations may emphasize low-carbon healthcare operations (mitigation/resource efficiency) rather than resilience; classification here assumes the mechanism’s defining endpoint is institutional adaptive capacity to climate-related health risks.
co_benefits: General public health system strengthening; improved routine healthcare access/quality; progress on broader sustainable development objectives (non-climate).
instance_edge_case_notes: The instrument could also be interpreted as a high-level planning/strategy commitment, but the phrasing is primarily a quantified implementation target (“100%… implemented”) without specifying the plan, measures, or investments. Climate linkage is inferred (health-system resilience) rather than explicit.
